text: Not all glucose delivery is equal. The glycemic index — a measure of how rapidly carbohydrates raise blood sugar — shapes the quality of cognitive fuel as much as its quantity. High-glycemic foods spike glucose sharply, triggering an insulin surge that can overshoot and leave the brain temporarily under-fueled. Nutritional neuroscientist Fernando Gomez-Pinilla at UCLA has shown that diets chronically high in refined sugars impair synaptic plasticity and reduce BDNF, the protein that supports learning and memory. A steady supply from low-glycemic sources — legumes, oats, leafy vegetables — provides the gradual, sustained energy that focused cognition demands.
question: According to the passage, what effect do diets high in refined sugars have on the brain, according to Fernando Gomez-Pinilla's research?

options.text: They increase the production of BDNF
They stabilize insulin levels over time
They impair synaptic plasticity and reduce BDNF
They enhance working memory in the short term
